sentences: Texas A&M Aggies (variously A&M or Texas Aggies) refers to the students, graduates, and sports teams of Texas A&M University.
The nickname "Aggie" was once common at land-grant or "Ag" (agriculture) schools in many states.
The teams compete in Division I of NCAA sports.
Texas A&M was a charter member of the Southwest Conference until its dissolution and subsequent formation of the Big 12 Conference in 1996.
The Aggies competed in the Big 12 until joining the Southeastern Conference (SEC) on July 1, 2012.
Texas A&M's official school colors are maroon and white.
The teams are referred to as Aggies and the mascot is a Rough Collie named Reveille.
The 2016 Big 12 Conference Baseball Tournament was held from May 25 through May 29 at Chickasaw Bricktown Ballpark in Oklahoma City, Oklahoma.
The annual tournament determined the conference champion of the Division I Big 12 Conference for college baseball.
The TCU Horned Frogs won the Tournament Championship, and as the winner of the tournament, TCU earned the league's automatic bid to the 2016 NCAA Division I Baseball Tournament.
The Big 12 Conference Baseball Tournament (sometimes known simply as the Big 12 Tournament) is the conference championship tournament in baseball for the Big 12 Conference.
Going back to the original format abandoned in 2005, the tournament consists of two separate four-team double-elimination tournaments.
The winners of each of those tournament face each other in a one-game match for the championship.
The winner receives the conference's automatic bid to the NCAA Division I Baseball Tournament.
The 2007 Big 12 Conference Baseball Tournament was held at AT&T Bricktown Ballpark in Oklahoma City, OK from May 23 through May 27.
Texas A&M won the tournament and earned the Big 12 Conference's automatic bid to the 2007 NCAA Division I Baseball Tournament.
This was the second year the conference used the round robin tournament setup.
The winners of each group at the end of the round robin faced each other in a one-game match for the championship.
The 1997 Big 12 Conference Baseball Tournament was the first in Big 12 history, and the only one to be held at All Sports Stadium in Oklahoma City, OK from May 15th through May 18th.
Oklahoma won the inaugural tournament and earned the Big 12 Conference's automatic bid to the 1997 NCAA Division I Baseball Tournament.
The format followed that used by the NCAA Division I Baseball Championship at the time: a six-team, double-elimination tournament.
The 1998 Big 12 Conference Baseball Tournament was the second played in Big 12 history, and the first to be held at AT&T Bricktown Ballpark in Oklahoma City, OK from May 14 through May 17.
Texas Tech won the tournament and earned the Big 12 Conference's automatic bid to the 1998 NCAA Division I Baseball Tournament.
The format followed that used by the NCAA Division I Baseball Championship at the time: a six-team, double-elimination tournament.
The 2007 Big East Conference Baseball Tournament was held at KeySpan Park in Brooklyn, NY.
This was the twenty third annual Big East Conference Baseball Tournament.
The won their third tournament championship and claimed the Big East Conference's automatic bid to the 2007 NCAA Division I Baseball Tournament.
The 2015 Big 12 Conference Baseball Tournament was held from May 20 through May 24 at ONEOK Field in Tulsa, Oklahoma.
The annual tournament determines the conference champion of the Division I Big 12 Conference for college baseball.
The winner of the tournament will earn the league's automatic bid to the 2015 NCAA Division I Baseball Tournament.
The 2017 Big 12 Conference Baseball Tournament will be held from May 24 through May 28 at Chickasaw Bricktown Ballpark in Oklahoma City, Oklahoma.
The annual tournament determines the conference champion of the Division I Big 12 Conference for college baseball.
The winner of the tournament will earn the league's automatic bid to the 2017 NCAA Division I Baseball Tournament.
The 1996 Big East Conference Baseball Tournament was held at Senator Thomas J. Dodd Memorial Stadium in Norwich, CT.
This was the twelfth annual Big East Conference Baseball Tournament.
The won their only tournament championship in their first year in the league, and claimed the Big East Conference's automatic bid to the 1996 NCAA Division I Baseball Tournament.
West Virginia joined the Big 12 Conference after the 2012 season.

candidate.summarize1.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `passages`, produce the field `summary`.

Role: This agent summarizes first-hop evidence and exposes the bridge entity, any missing fact(s), ambiguity, and concrete next-hop retrieval directions. Do not attempt to produce the final answer—the summary should tell the next agent exactly what to fetch to finish the multi-hop chain.

Output requirements (single `summary` value only):
- Start the summary with the header: First-hop findings:
- Include these labeled parts (use a short bullet list or 2–4 concise sentences; keep overall output brief):
  1) Evidence found: For each person/entity named in the question, list which passage(s) mention matching candidate pages/titles and quote any explicit fact tokens found there (names, birth years, publication years, short parenthetical notes). Keep quoted fragments <=15 words and cite the passage title/label for each fact.
  2) Ambiguities: If a name yields multiple candidate pages or variants in the passages, explicitly enumerate those candidate titles/labels as they appear; if an expected page/variant is missing, note that too.
  3) Bridge entity / Missing fact: Explicitly name the bridge entity exactly as it appears in the passages (the person, work, or organization that links to the next hop) and state the precise missing fact needed to answer the question (e.g., a birth date, founding year, canonical series ordering).
  4) Retrieval direction: Provide 2–4 concrete, prioritized next-hop targets (specific page titles or disambiguation pages to fetch, and/or exact sections to check such as lede/infobox/Career/History). Include short example search keywords or one-line instructions (e.g., "open '<Name>' page and check lede/infobox and 'Career' section for 'Italian champion' year").
- End with a brief explicit note that the final answer is deferred until those pages are retrieved.

Hard constraints (do not violate):
- Do not provide a final comparison or answer here. Do not guess or invent facts not present in the provided passages or introduce outside knowledge.
- Do not assume which candidate is intended when multiple candidates exist.
- Avoid long verbatim copying of passages (>15 words).
- Preserve concise factual phrasing and verbatim tokens that appear in the passages (names, dates, page titles) when reporting evidence.

Keep the summary concise and focused—just the first-hop findings needed to guide the next retrieval hop.
candidate.create_query_hop2.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `summary_1`, produce the fields `query`.

Output contract:
- Produce exactly one field named `query` whose value is a single short retrieval query string (one line, no surrounding explanation or extra fields).
- Do not answer, summarize, or explain anything in `query`. Do not include fallback instructions or user-facing requests for more info in this field.

Construction rules (apply the most specific rule that fits the inputs):
1) Linking two named works: If `question` or `summary_1` names two or more specific works (songs, tracks, titles) and the missing evidence is the bridge between them, emit a concise interrogative that names both works and requests the linking evidence (artist / album / recording). Keep it short (about ≤ 12 words). Example form: "Which artist or album recorded both 'Work A' and 'Work B'?"
2) Missing album artist/genre: If `summary_1` indicates the album title is present but the artist or genre is unknown, emit a compact search string of the form: <AlbumTitle> album artist genre
   - No punctuation, no question phrasing, no extra words. Example: "Levitate album artist genre"
3) Sports season-year lookups: If the `question` contains a specific season-year token and the missing bridge is a league-season page, emit a short query that includes the year token, a league identifier (e.g., "NHL" or "National Hockey League"), and a cue such as "regular season" or "season count". Keep it concise (≤ ~10 tokens).
4) Default: Otherwise, produce one concise search-style query (≤ ~12 words) that uses visible tokens from `question` and `summary_1` targeted at retrieving the missing supporting evidence (artist, album, recording, genre, season page, etc.).

General guidance:
- Keep queries compact; avoid lists, long page titles, or copying large spans from hidden targets.
- Do not invent facts or candidate answers; use only visible tokens or neutral retrieval cues.
- Prefer a short search string; use an interrogative only for the two-works linking case in rule 1.
- The goal is to maximize recovery of the missing bridge evidence for the downstream retriever.

Return only the single `query` field.
candidate.summarize2.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `context`, `passages`, produce the field `summary`.

Primary function: integrate the newly retrieved `passages` (second-hop evidence) with the provided `context` (first-hop) so the output is a grounded post-retrieval state that preserves the facts the final-answer generator needs. Resolve the earlier bridge when the supplied passages support doing so; retain uncertainty only when evidence is insufficient.

Behavior rules (minimal, general):
1) Preserve concise-answer behavior. If `context` already contains a concise direct answer (a short sentence or phrase), keep that text exactly as the first sentence of `summary`, unless it contradicts the `passages`.
2) When `passages` supply additional, directly supported facts about entities named in the `question` or `context`, append up to one brief supporting sentence per distinct named entity (keep total `summary` to 1–3 sentences, preferably 1–2). Each supporting sentence should: start with the entity's full name as shown in the passages, include a short paraphrased attribution drawn only from the passages (no more than ~10–12 words) such as nationality, a notable achievement, a birth date, or a current affiliation, and avoid inventing or inferring facts not present in the inputs.
3) When an object/entity page (for example, a car page, group page, or similar) lists associated people and a separate passage provides a biography for one of those listed people, produce a 1–2 sentence integrated summary: (a) first sentence: name the people as listed on the object page (e.g., "driven by X and Y" or "member of Group Z"); (b) optional second short clause/sentence: paraphrase key biographical facts from the person's passage (only facts present there — e.g., nationality, notable wins/awards, birth date, current team/affiliation). Keep wording compact and factual.
4) Ground everything in the provided `passages` and `context`. Do not add new claims, dates, roles, or affiliations not explicitly supported. Avoid long verbatim quotations from the passages; you may paraphrase short phrases (≤10 words) only if they appear in the sources, and you may copy short strings such as names or dates exactly as shown.
5) If the passages do not provide enough evidence to resolve the bridge, state that the passages do not specify the requested fact while still listing any supported names or facts. Do not guess or fabricate missing information.
6) Output contract: return only the field `summary` containing 1–3 concise sentences (preferably 1–2) that integrate the first-hop `context` and second-hop `passages` according to the rules above. Do not add commentary, answer-labels, or extraneous text.
candidate.final_answer.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `summary_1`, and `summary_2`, produce the field `answer`.

Instructions:
- Use only the visible summaries (summary_1 and summary_2) together with the question as your evidence. Do not introduce external facts, sources, or claims not present in those summaries.
- Treat summary_2 (the later, integrated post-retrieval state) as primary evidence while still considering summary_1. Do not assume the final state is unresolved simply because summary_1 describes an earlier unresolved bridge; rely on the combined visible summaries to reach a conclusion.
- If summary_1 and summary_2 both clearly and unambiguously support the same binary (yes/no) conclusion to the question, then the value of the `answer` field must be exactly one lowercase token: either "yes" (if both summaries affirm the fact) or "no" (if both summaries deny the fact). The `answer` field must contain only that single token and nothing else (no punctuation, no capitalization, no explanation, no additional words).
- If the summaries conflict, are ambiguous, or do not together provide a clear binary yes/no verdict, produce a concise answer string in the `answer` field that best reflects what the summaries support. This concise answer may be a short phrase or sentence, but must still be supported solely by the summaries and must avoid adding new facts.
- Always minimize verbosity: prefer the single-token binary response when the summaries unambiguously agree; otherwise keep the answer brief and directly tied to the visible summaries.

Produce only the `answer` field as output.

response.summary_1:
First-hop findings:
- Evidence found: Passage [1] ("2007 Big 12 Conference Baseball Tournament") explicitly states "Texas A&M won the tournament" (passage [1]). The tournament itself is identified as the "2007 Big 12 Conference Baseball Tournament" (passage [1]).
- Ambiguities: The provided passages do not include any page about Texas A&M's historical conference memberships or a "Southwest Conference" membership list. No alternative winner candidates appear in the passages, and no Texas A&M page/variant (e.g., "Texas A&M Aggies", "Texas A&M University") with membership history is present.
- Bridge entity / Missing fact: Bridge entity = "Texas A&M" (as written in passage [1]). Missing fact needed = the name of the conference Texas A&M belonged to before 1996 (and the relevant membership years).
- Retrieval direction (prioritized):
  1) Open "Texas A&M University" (or "Texas A&M Aggies") page — check the lede/infobox and the "Athletics" or "History"/"Conference affiliations" sections for pre-1996 conference membership. Example search: "Texas A&M conference history pre-1996" or "Texas A&M conference affiliations".
  2) Open "Texas A&M Aggies baseball" (or athletics program) page — check lede/infobox and "History" for baseball-specific conference membership prior to 1996.
  3) Open "Southwest Conference" page — check the lede and "Members" or "Membership history" sections to confirm member schools and years.
  4) If present, open any disambiguation/roster pages like "Texas A&M athletics conference affiliations" or "List of Texas A&M Aggies baseball seasons" and check membership notes.
Final answer is deferred until those pages/sections are retrieved.
hop2_docs: 1996 Southwest Conference Baseball Tournament | The 1996 Southwest Conference Baseball Tournament was the league's annual postseason tournament used to determine the Southwest Conference's (SWC) automatic bid to the 1996 NCAA Division I Baseball Tournament.  The tournament was held from May 15 through May 18 at Dan Law Field on the campus of Texas Tech University in Lubbock, Texas and drew a total attendance of 31,542.  This was the first time the SWC Tournament was held in Lubbock, and the final conference tournament before the conference disbanded.

1996 Kansas Jayhawks football team | The 1996 Kansas Jayhawks football team represented the University of Kansas in the 1996 NCAA Division I-A football season.  They were coached by head coach Glen Mason, who resigned after the season to become the head coach at Minnesota and played their home games at Memorial Stadium in Lawrence, Kansas.  It was the Jayhawks first year in the newly formed Big 12 Conference.  The Big 12 conference was formed by the eight teams of the recently dissolved Big Eight Conference and was joined by Baylor, Texas, Texas A&M, and Texas Tech, all formally of the Southwest Conference which had dissolved following the 1995–1996 school year as well.

1996–97 Iowa State Cyclones men's basketball team | The 1996–97 Iowa State Cyclones men's basketball team represented Iowa State University during the 1996–97 NCAA Division I men's basketball season.  The Cyclones were coached by Tim Floyd, who was in his 3rd season.  They played their home games at Hilton Coliseum in Ames, Iowa and is an original member of the newly created Big 12 Conference.  The Big 12 conference was formed by the eight teams of the recently dissolved Big Eight Conference and was joined by Baylor, Texas, Texas A&M, and Texas Tech, all formally of the Southwest Conference which had also dissolved following the 1995–1996 school year.

Baylor–Texas Tech football rivalry | The Baylor–Texas Tech football rivalry is an American college football rivalry between the Baylor Bears and Texas Tech Red Raiders.  Each school is a member of the Big 12 Conference.  The rivalry began in 1929.  The game has been played every year since 1956 despite the fact that Texas Tech was a member of the Border Intercollegiate Athletic Conference.  In 1960, Texas Tech joined the Southwest Conference, ensuring the rivalry would continue.  In 1996, the Southwest Conference dissolved, and both teams were invited, along with the Texas Longhorns and Texas A&M Aggies, with former members of the Big Eight Conference to form the Big 12 Conference.  From 1947–64, Baylor won 14 of the 15 games.  From 1996–2010, Texas Tech won 15 straight games.  In the most recent meeting of the two teams, Texas Tech snapped Baylor's 5 game win streak to bring the series record to a tie.

1996–97 Kansas Jayhawks men's basketball team | The 1996–97 Kansas Jayhawks men's basketball team represented the University of Kansas in the 1996-97 NCAA Division I men's basketball season, which was the Jayhawks' 99th basketball season.  The head coach was Roy Williams, who served his 9th year at KU.  The team played its home games in Allen Fieldhouse in Lawrence, Kansas.  It was the Jayhawks first year in the newly formed Big 12 Conference.  The Big 12 conference was formed by the eight teams of the recently dissolved Big Eight Conference and was joined by Baylor, Texas, Texas A&M, and Texas Tech, all formally of the Southwest Conference which had dissolved following the 1995–1996 school year as well.

1996 Texas Longhorns football team | The 1996 Texas Longhorns football team represented the University of Texas at Austin during the 1996 NCAA Division I-A football season.  They were represented in the Big 12 Conference in the South Division in its first season in existence.  They played their home games at Darrell K Royal–Texas Memorial Stadium in Austin, Texas.  The team was led by head coach John Mackovic.

Texas Tech Red Raiders cross country | The Texas Tech Red Raiders men's and women's cross country teams represent Texas Tech University, often referred to as Texas Tech.  The teams compete as members of the Big 12 Conference in the National Collegiate Athletic Association (NCAA).  The women's cross country team is only one of three teams to win the Big 12 Conference title since the conference was formed in 1996.

Texas Tech Red Raiders cross country
